title: Records, Index Entries, and Metadata Relating to Shapoor Reporter, Ardeshir Reporter, and Iran (1950–1960) (Central Intelligence Agency)

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

Sir Shapoor Ardeshirji Reporter (1921–2013), including but not limited to the variants “Shapoor Reporter,” “Shapour Reporter,” “Sir Shapoor Reporter,” “Sir Shapour Reporter,” “S. Reporter,” and “S. A. Reporter.”

This request is limited to the period from 1 January 1950 through 31 December 1960.

This request seeks records in which this individual is named, referenced, indexed, described, or assessed, whether as a principal subject or as an incidental contact, intermediary, liaison, introducer, adviser, or participant in diplomatic, intelligence, or political activity relating to Iran.

To ensure clarity and completeness, I am requesting the following categories of records:

First, substantive records. This includes intelligence reports, cables, memoranda, diplomatic correspondence, briefing papers, analytical products, situation reports, and internal summaries that mention or refer to Shapoor Reporter.

Second, communications and liaison records. This includes records of contact, meetings, coordination, or exchanges involving U.S., British, or Iranian officials in which Shapoor Reporter is referenced.

Third, administrative and reference records. This includes file jackets, routing slips, registry entries, cross-reference sheets, dissemination records, and other materials that identify or describe records concerning Shapoor Reporter.

Fourth, index and metadata records. This includes name traces, index entries, database search results, file titles, file numbers, date ranges, originating offices, and any catalog or archival metadata sufficient to identify the existence and structure of responsive records, even where underlying documents are withheld.

Fifth, contextual records. This includes records in which Shapoor Reporter is referenced in connection with one or more of the following: Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, the Shah of Iran; Iranian court politics; Anglo-American coordination in Iran; British diplomatic or intelligence personnel; and political developments surrounding the 1953 coup.

If responsive records are withheld in whole or in part, I request that your agency provide, to the maximum extent permitted by law, the file title, file number or identifier, date or date range, originating office, and a brief description sufficient to understand the nature of the record. I also request that all reasonably segregable non-exempt material be released and that any exemptions be specifically identified.

If any responsive records have been transferred to or are held by another agency or by the National Archives and Records Administration, I request that this request be referred or that you provide the relevant transfer, accession, or file reference information.

If this request is deemed too broad, please process it in the following order of priority:

1. Index entries, name traces, and metadata (1950–1960)  
2. Records explicitly naming Shapoor Reporter (1950–1960)  
3. Records linking Shapoor Reporter to Iran, the Shah, or British liaison activity  


Thank you for your consideration.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Adil Minocherhomjee

title: FBI RB 4.6.26

communications.communication:
Dear FOIA Officer:

You are receiving this correspondence as a request under the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. subsection 552. I am requesting the following:

Copies of all Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) records, documents, and communications responsive to Raymond Augustine Bauer, aka Raymond A. Bauer, aka Raymond Bauer (September 7, 1916-July 10, 1977; SSN 321-05-3494 ).

Dr. Bauer was a professor of the behavioral sciences at Harvard University and holder of the Joseph C. Wilson professorship at the Harvard School of Business Administration. Dr. Bauer was among the first of the Cold War Sovietologists, writing extensively on the social structure and psychology of the Soviet Union in books like "The New Man in Soviet Psychology" and "How the Soviet System Works".

It is public knowledge that the FBI has a file on Dr. Bauer: the historian David C. Engerman identified its existence in his 2009 book "Know Your Enemy: The Rise and Fall of America's Soviet Experts". Writing on page 58, he states: "Yet the very source of this friend’s recollection indicates the problem at hand: it is among the nearly 200 pages of Bauer’s FBI file. Bauer faced a major security inquisition in 1954, forcing him to abandon the prospect of doing the kind of classified consulting that [Clyde] Kluckhohn and [Alex] Inkeles did. He defended his college record by telling his boss that he joined the [American Student Union] in order to fight Communist control, but at the same time worried that 'a careless reader might interpret' an article in the student newspaper 'as a ‘Party-line’ argument.' After leaving Northwestern, Bauer joined the U.S. Navy and was soon sent to the navy’s Oriental Languages School in Colorado. His navy experience, too, concerned security investigators; he had been friendly with a classmate, William Remington, who was later convicted of perjury for lying about his membership in the Communist Party. Such a friendship was not a crime even in those years, but added to suspicions about Bauer. As if the ASU and a brush with an actual Communist were not enough of a problem for the FBI, Bauer joined the American Veterans Committee at Harvard just after the war, staying with the group until its collapse."

The FBI's FOIA logs confirm Dr. Engerman filed FOIPA request 982461-000 on 8/21/03, and was granted a partial release on 4/30/04. 

This file was also requested by communications historian Christopher Simpson on 10/4/89, via FOIPA request 321078-D00, according to the FBI's FOIA logs. Although this request was administratively closed on 4/24/90 because it was a duplicate request, it demonstrates ongoing interest in Dr. Bauer as a subject of public interest.

Proof of death is not required as Dr. Bauer's date of birth was over 100 years ago; however, Dr. Bauer's obituary appeared in the New York Times on July 11, 1977. A copy can be found here: https://www.nytimes.com/1977/07/11/archives/raymond-bauer-60-business-professor-taught-at-harvard.html

I am asking you waive any fees associated with this request. I am a member of the news media. My bylines, which include Unicorn Riot, Covert Action Magazine, and the Geopolitical Economy Report, can be found in both print and electronic publications, and I am sending this request in coordination with Muckrock, an award-winning and nationally-recognized news organization. Please take note of the Office of Management and Budget guidelines published March 27, 1987 (52 FR 10012) that include electronic publications and other nontraditional publishers as representatives of the news media.

A fee waiver is appropriate in situations where "disclosure of the requested information is in the public interest because it is likely to contribute significantly to public understanding of the operations and activities of the government, and is not primarily in the commercial interest of the requester,” 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(4)(iii). At various points in time, Dr. Bauer worked overtly or covertly for the government, conducting Cold War era social science research. He held a fellowship at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology's Center for International Studies (CENIS) while writing "The New Man in Soviet Psychology"; CENIS was covertly funded by the Central Intelligence Agency, according to journalists David Wise and Thomas Ross in their 1965 book "The Invisible Government." Any FBI records relevant to Dr. Bauer would contribute significantly to public understanding of how the FBI handled security investigations into academics with ties to the military and intelligence community during the Cold War. Per Dr. Engerman, the FBI's investigation reviewed alleged ties to communism, and given Dr. Bauer's role as one of the foremost Sovietologists of the early 1950s, any investigation would be of public interest.

Additionally, these records have already been released on at least one occasion, to Dr. Engerman, so there should be no issue with releasing them to other parties.

This request is not being made for commercial purposes. All documents will be provided to the general public without charge.

Please conduct a search of the Central Records System, including but not limited to the Electronic Surveillance (ELSUR) Records System, the Microphone Surveillance (MISUR) Indices, the Physical Surveillance (FISUR) Indices, and the Technical Surveillance (TESUR) Indices, for both main-file records and cross-reference records related to the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Center for International Studies, United States Air Force, Human Resources Research Institute, and Harvard University.

In the event that there are fees, I am willing to pay up to $100. Please advise if fees will exceed this amount so I might make an arrangement for requesting the records. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

I look forward to your response within 20 working days, as the statute requires. If access to the records I am requesting will take longer, please contact me with information about when I might expect copies or the ability to inspect the requested records.

If you deny any or all of this request, please cite each specific exemption you feel justifies the refusal to release the information and notify me of the appeal procedures available to me under the law.

Sincerely,

Robert Skvarla, Jr.

title: California Public Records Act Request — Riverside County, CA. Body-Worn Camera Footage, Officer-Involved Shootings, July 1st-31st 2025

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the California Public Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

I hereby formally request access to and copies of the following public records maintained by the Riverside County Sheriff's Department:

All body-worn camera (BWC) video and audio recordings from any and all deputies, officers, or law enforcement personnel involved in or present at any officer-involved shooting (OIS) incident that occurred within Riverside County Sheriff's Department jurisdiction between July 1st 2025 and July 31st 2025, inclusive.

I am requesting the following records for each qualifying officer-involved shooting incident occurring within the stated date range:

1. All body-worn camera video footage from deputies who discharged a firearm during the incident.
2. All body-worn camera video footage from any deputies who were present on scene during or immediately following the discharge of a firearm, including assisting and backup officers.
3. All body-worn camera video footage capturing the period immediately preceding, during, and following each incident, including approach, engagement, and post-incident medical response.
4. All corresponding dashcam or in-car video footage from patrol vehicles present at the scene of each incident.
5. All related CAD (Computer-Aided Dispatch) audio and dispatch recordings pertaining to each incident.
6. All incident or crime reports directly associated with each qualifying officer-involved shooting, to the extent disclosable under SB 1421 and existing CPRA law.

Legal Basis for Disclosure
This request is supported by multiple provisions of California law that specifically mandate the disclosure of officer-involved shooting records:

Senate Bill 1421 (Penal Code § 832.7(b)(1)(A)) requires disclosure of records relating to incidents in which a peace officer discharged a firearm at a person, regardless of whether the shooting resulted in death or injury.
Assembly Bill 748 (Gov. Code § 6254(f)(4)) requires law enforcement agencies to release video and audio recordings of "critical incidents," defined to include any incident in which an officer discharges a firearm at a person, within 45 days of the incident, unless the agency can demonstrate by clear and convincing evidence that disclosure would substantially interfere with an active criminal or administrative investigation.
The California Supreme Court has held that agencies may not impose unreasonable fees or use cost as a barrier to the disclosure of records mandated under SB 1421 and AB 748 (City of Eureka v. Superior Court, 2020).

Should any portion of a requested record be withheld, I respectfully request that you comply with Gov. Code § 7922.000 and provide a written explanation specifying the precise legal exemption or privilege claimed for each withheld item, as well as a detailed description of the nature of the record being withheld sufficient to assess the applicability of the claimed exemption.

Format and Delivery
I request that all responsive records be produced in their original, unaltered digital format (e.g., MP4, AVI, or the native format used by the department's body-worn camera system).

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 calendar days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

James Preston

title: Florida Sunshine Law Request — Pinellas County, FL. Body-Worn Camera Footage, Officer-Involved Shootings, July 1, 2024 – December 31, 2024

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Florida Sunshine Law, I hereby request the following records:

All body-worn camera (BWC) video and audio recordings from any and all deputies or law enforcement personnel involved in or present at any officer-involved shooting (OIS) or deputy-involved shooting (DIS) incident that occurred within Pinellas County Sheriff's Office jurisdiction between July 1, 2024 and December 31, 2024, inclusive.

Specific Records Requested
For each qualifying incident within the stated date range, I am requesting:

1. All body-worn camera video footage from deputies who discharged a firearm during the incident.
2. All body-worn camera video footage from any deputies present on scene during or immediately following the discharge of a firearm, including assisting, backup, and responding officers.
3. All body-worn camera footage capturing the period immediately preceding, during, and following each incident — including initial approach, commands given, engagement, and post-incident medical response.
4. All corresponding dashcam or in-car video footage from patrol vehicles present at or responding to the scene.
5. All Computer-Aided Dispatch (CAD) logs and dispatch audio recordings associated with each qualifying incident.
6. All available incident reports, offense reports, or narrative summaries associated with each qualifying OIS incident, to the extent not exempt under § 119.071, F.S.
7. The full name, badge number, and years of service of involved deputy/deputies, to the extent disclosable under Florida law.
8. Any press release, official statement, or public communication issued by the Pinellas County Sheriff's Office in connection with each qualifying incident.

Legal Basis
Under Chapter 119.07(1)(a), Florida Statutes, every person has the right to inspect and copy public records. Law enforcement recordings and incident documentation created in the course of official duties are public records under Florida law. Florida maintains a strong presumption in favor of public access, and any exemptions must be narrowly construed.

Should any portion of the requested records be withheld, I respectfully request that you identify each document withheld, cite the specific statutory exemption relied upon for each withheld item, and provide all non-exempt, reasonably segregable portions of any partially exempt records pursuant to § 119.07(1)(d), F.S.

Format and Delivery
I request all responsive records be produced in their original, unaltered digital format (e.g., MP4, AVI, or the native format used by the department's body-worn camera system).

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 business days.

title: CalGang (San Diego County Sheriff's Department)

communications.subject: California Public Records Act Request: CalGang (San Diego County Sheriff's Department)
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the California Public Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

Hello, 

A memoradnum of understanding is required for outside agencies to access CalGang databases. I am requesting the MOUs between this department and ICE and Customs and Border Protection (CBP). I am requesting copies of those requests from 2016 to today. Along with the information below, for the same time frames. 

An Agency that is interested in accessing the CalGang database shall submit a written
request to the Department or the local Node Administrator on the Agency’s letterhead. The
written request shall provide the following:
(1) A reason for its need to access the CalGang database.
(A) The Agency shall demonstrate a need to know and a right to know to satisfy
subdivision (e)(1).
(2) The number of Users who will be accessing the CalGang database at that Agency and
the level of access that is requested for each User.
(3) The Agency’s assigned originating agency identifier (ORI) number, if applicable.
(4) The addresses of the facilities within the Agency where Users will access the
CalGang database.
(5) The name and contact information for the person(s) designated by the Agency as the
point of contact and training coordinator. If the Agency’s point of contact and training
coordinator are two different persons, the Agency shall identify both.
(6) If data entry access is requested, the Agency shall identify the classification(s),
rank(s), and related expertise of the person(s) who will be responsible for performing the
supervisory review required by sections 752.8 and 753.4; and describe the organizational
structure and process through which the supervisory review process required by sections
752.8 and 753.4 will occur.


Thank you

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 calendar days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Joey Scott

title: Law enforcement informal and formal settlement data and reports (Washoe County Sheriffs Office)

communications.subject: Nevada Public Records Act Request: Law enforcement informal and formal settlement data and reports (Washoe County Sheriffs Office)
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Nevada Public Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

Materials related to and sufficient to show all instances of alleged or actual inappropriate or illegal behavior or actions resulting in compensation or expenditures involving this agency. 

Responsive materials include records of all settlements (formal and informal) and payments to which this agency was or is a party for the period from January 1, 2020 through the date this request is processed. Responsive materials include those for matters and legal claims resolved prior to the initiation of any litigation. 

In particular, materials responsive to this request include all reports, lists, databases, spreadsheets, and other documentation detailing incidents of legal claims or other allegations requiring compensation for claimants, attorneys, or any other party. 

If spreadsheets or similar documents are available in response to this request, please provide all segregable data, including but not limited to:

The nature of the claim or legal matter
The claimant and other parties involved
The relevant case and tracking numbers for court and internal systems
The date the lawsuit was served or litigation commenced
The final disposition or current status of the case, including whether a case is still pending
The amount spent to date as a result of the legal engagement
The amount paid to any parties related to the legal claim, including any city payout and any non-monetary compensation

If clarification of this request is required, please respond to the provided email address or leave a message at 617-299-1832.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 5 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Annemarie Grant

title: Caltrans: ADA Grievance Records and Tracking Logs

communications.subject: California Public Records Act Request: Caltrans: ADA Grievance Records and Tracking Logs
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the California Public Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

I request the opportunity to inspect the following records. If these records exist in electronic format, I request that they be provided in their native or searchable electronic format (e.g., Excel, CSV, or searchable PDF) pursuant to Government Code § 7922.570, which shall serve as my inspection of the records. As citizen investigating state-level ADA compliance and grievance procedures, I am specifically interested in the volume, handling, and resolution of complaints submitted via the Caltrans online portal and the ada.compliance.office@dot.ca.gov email address.

Records Requested

Case Management Log/Database Export: A digital export of the tracking log or database used to manage ADA grievances. Based on recent test filings, I am aware that Caltrans issues sequential case numbers (e.g., Case #11963). I am requesting a list of all cases filed from January 1, 2022, to the present, including the following fields:

Case Number

Date Received

Subject/Nature of Complaint (e.g., "Inaccessible Curb Ramp," "Website Accessibility")

Location (District, County, Route, or Postmile if applicable)

Current Status (Open, Closed, Pending)

Date of Resolution/Closure

Photographs (if applicable) 


Aggregated Reporting: Any annual or quarterly summary reports generated by the ADA Compliance Office regarding the total number of grievances received, the average time to resolution, and the geographic distribution of complaints.

Privacy and Redaction (California Govt. Code § 7927.700)
In the interest of protecting individual privacy and expediting this request, I am NOT requesting any "Personally Identifiable Information" (PII). Please redact the following information from the responsive records:

Names of complainants.

Home addresses, personal phone numbers, or personal email addresses of complainants.

Specific medical information or descriptions of individual disabilities.

I am requesting only the substance of the grievance (the infrastructure or service failure) and the administrative handling of the file.

Production of Records
If you contend that any portion of these records is exempt from disclosure, please provide a written response citing the specific statutory exemption and a brief explanation of how the exemption applies to the withheld information.

Thank you for the state of California's dedication to transparency, and to Caltran's dedication to fulfilling the promise of the Americans with Disabilities Act.
